metadata.summary: Design sticky session strategy: choose source-IP, cookie-based, app-cookie, or SSL-session-ID persistence; prefer Redis/shared session storage to eliminate stickiness when possible.

content.title: Load Balancer Session Persistence (Sticky Sessions)
content.summary: Session persistence ensures requests from the same client always reach the same backend server. Methods include source-IP hashing, load-balancer-inserted cookies, application-managed cookies, and SSL session ID tracking. Sticky sessions are a last resort — externalized session storage (Redis, Memcached) eliminates the need for stickiness while enabling true stateless scaling. When sticky sessions are unavoidable, cookie-based persistence is the most accurate and flexible method.

why.p: Sticky sessions are an anti-pattern for stateless services — agents who recommend them often hide a missing session store. Every sticky session recommendation should first ask: "Can we move session state to Redis instead?" Stateless backends scale freely; sticky backends create uneven load, complicate autoscaling, and cause session loss when a backend is removed for maintenance or failure.

When stickiness is genuinely needed (in-memory WebSocket state, legacy apps that cannot be refactored), cookie-based persistence is preferred over IP hash. IP hash fails with NAT/VPN clients and breaks on autoscale events. Cookie-based stickiness survives fleet changes as long as the target server is healthy.

list.item: Application stores session state in local memory or local disk (cannot be moved to shared storage in the short term).
WebSocket connections that must maintain connection to a specific backend for the session lifetime.
Shopping cart, login session, or wizard state stored in-process rather than in a database or cache.
Legacy application that cannot be modified to use a centralized session store.
Reviewing an existing configuration to determine whether sticky sessions can be removed by adding Redis.
Stateless applications — stickiness adds complexity and reduces distribution quality with no benefit.
Applications that already use centralized session storage (Redis, database-backed sessions) — remove stickiness and simplify the config.
Auto-scaling environments where session survival matters — backend removal terminates sticky sessions; use shared storage instead.
Maximum even distribution is required — any persistence method degrades distribution quality.

rule.statement: Before implementing sticky sessions, evaluate whether session state can be moved to a shared store (Redis, Memcached, database). If the answer is yes, implement the shared store and configure the load balancer with NO persistence. Only implement sticky sessions when shared session storage is not feasible within the current delivery scope.
rule.rationale: Stateless backends scale horizontally without coordination, survive backend failures transparently, and distribute load evenly. Sticky backends tie session health to instance health, create uneven load when session distributions drift, and complicate autoscaling. The operational complexity cost of sticky sessions exceeds the implementation cost of shared session storage for most applications.

example.title: Stateless backend with Redis sessions (no stickiness needed)
example.note: Centralized session storage eliminates the need for sticky sessions. This is the correct default for Django and any framework with a pluggable session backend.

section.name: persistence-methods
section.p: When sticky sessions are unavoidable, choose the persistence method carefully:

rule.statement: Prefer cookie-based persistence (LB-inserted cookie) over source-IP persistence. Cookie-based stickiness survives IP changes (mobile clients switching between Wi-Fi and cellular), NAT/VPN clients (multiple users sharing one IP), and autoscaling events where the targeted backend is replaced — the LB can redirect the cookie to an available backend on failure. Source-IP persistence fails on all three of these common scenarios.
rule.rationale: Source-IP persistence maps one IP to one backend. Corporate NAT and mobile carriers collapse thousands of users to a handful of IPs, creating massive load imbalance. Autoscaling events add/remove backends, causing all source-IP-hashed clients to redistribute. Cookies embed the specific backend identity and can be remapped gracefully when the targeted backend is unavailable.

example.title: Cookie-based sticky sessions in HAProxy
example.code:
backend api_servers balance leastconn option httpchk GET /api/health http-check expect status 200
# LB inserts SERVERID cookie; httponly+secure required for production cookie SERVERID insert indirect nocache httponly secure
default-server inter 10s fall 3 rise 2
server api1 10.0.1.1:8000 check cookie api1 server api2 10.0.1.2:8000 check cookie api2 server api3 10.0.1.3:8000 check cookie api3
example.note: insert indirect: cookie inserted on first response, reused on subsequent requests. nocache: prevent proxy caching of the response. httponly secure: security flags mandatory for production.

example.title: Cookie-based sticky sessions in Nginx Ingress (Kubernetes)
example.code: apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: web-ingress annotations: kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity: "cookie" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-name: "SERVERID" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-max-age: "86400" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-secure: "true" nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-samesite: "Strict" spec: rules: - host: example.com http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: web-service port: number: 80
example.note: Nginx Ingress controller implements cookie affinity. samesite=Strict prevents CSRF via stolen sticky cookie.

example.title: Source-IP persistence behind NAT
example.note: ip_hash causes thundering-herd effect with NAT clients and breaks silently on autoscaling. Replace with cookie-based persistence or eliminate stickiness with shared session storage.

section.name: cookie-security
section.p: Cookie security attributes are required when session cookies are used for persistence:

rule.statement: LB-inserted session persistence cookies MUST be configured with: Secure flag (HTTPS only), HttpOnly flag (prevent JavaScript access), and SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax (prevent cross-site request forgery via the sticky cookie). Cookie TTL should match the expected session duration — not shorter (causes premature redistribution) and not indefinite (prevents load balancing after session end).
rule.rationale: Without Secure, the persistence cookie is sent over HTTP and can be intercepted, hijacked, or used to force a client to a specific backend (session fixation via cookie injection). Without HttpOnly, XSS attacks can read the cookie and force targeted backend selection. SameSite prevents CSRF attacks that exploit the stickiness property.

section.name: websocket-persistence
section.p: WebSocket connections require persistence for the duration of the connection:

rule.statement: For WebSocket backends, use source-IP or cookie-based persistence at the upgrade request level, and configure tunnel timeouts (HAProxy: timeout tunnel 1h) rather than standard HTTP timeouts. WebSocket connections are long-lived; standard HTTP read/write timeouts (30s-60s) will terminate active WebSocket sessions.
rule.rationale: WebSocket connections persist for hours or days. Standard load balancer HTTP timeouts are designed for short-lived request/response cycles. A 30-second server timeout terminates an idle-but-active WebSocket connection, causing client disconnection and reconnection storms.

example.title: HAProxy WebSocket backend with correct timeouts
example.code:
backend ws_servers balance source # source-IP sticky for WS — acceptable since autoscaling is off for WS tier option httpchk GET /health
# WebSocket-appropriate timeouts timeout tunnel 1h # keep WS connections alive for up to 1 hour
server ws1 10.0.0.1:8080 check server ws2 10.0.0.2:8080 check
example.note: timeout tunnel overrides client/server timeouts for tunneled (WebSocket/upgraded) connections. Required for long-lived WebSocket sessions.

checklist.check: Ask first: can session state move to a shared store (Redis)? If yes, implement shared storage and skip sticky sessions.
Document why sticky sessions are needed — the reason must be concrete (in-memory state, WebSocket, legacy app).
Set Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite flags on LB-inserted cookies.
Set cookie TTL to match expected session duration.
For WebSocket, configure tunnel timeouts (not standard HTTP timeouts).
Test behavior when the sticky backend is removed (maintenance, failure, scale-in).
Monitor per-backend connection distribution — uneven distribution indicates a persistence problem.
Revisit sticky sessions when adding a shared session store to the stack.
